sections.heading: Surgery
sections.content:
Radical inguinal orchiectomy is the gold standard for diagnosis and initial management of suspected testicular cancer. Retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (RPLND) serves both diagnostic and therapeutic roles for staging and treatment of retroperitoneal disease.

Principles:
1. Radical inguinal orchiectomy is the gold standard; trans-scrotal orchiectomy is discouraged due to scrotal violation associated with higher local recurrence rates and altered metastatic dissemination pathways [TEST-H].
2. When patient presents with rapidly increasing beta-hCG or AFP, metastatic disease on imaging, and symptoms of disseminated disease, chemotherapy can be initiated immediately without waiting for orchiectomy. However, radical inguinal orchiectomy should be performed at completion of chemotherapy [TEST-H].
3. Testis-sparing surgery (TSS) can be considered in select patients through inguinal approach with frozen section analysis [TEST-H].
4. Sperm banking should be discussed with reproductive age patients prior to any therapeutic intervention that may compromise fertility [TEST-H].
5. For equivocal mass <2 cm with normal AFP and beta-hCG, short-interval imaging can be considered prior to partial orchiectomy [TEST-H].
6. Use of testicular prosthesis should be discussed prior to radical inguinal orchiectomy [TEST-H].

Resectability criteria: RPLND is indicated for patients with metastatic NSGCT with residual retroperitoneal mass (>1 cm on axial imaging) following systemic chemotherapy and normalized post-chemotherapy serum tumor markers. For stage IIA nonseminoma with normal markers, RPLND is appropriate within 4 weeks of CT/MRI and within 2 weeks of confirmation of normal tumor markers. Nerve-sparing approaches should be prioritized to minimize ejaculatory disorders. Oncologic efficacy should never be compromised for maintenance of ejaculation [TEST-H]. For seminoma, RPLND can be considered for clinical stage IIA and non-bulky IIB disease. RPLND is not recommended for bulky (>3 cm) stage IIB seminoma [MS-13].

Pre-operative optimization: Fertility counseling and sperm banking should be considered prior to RPLND. Referral to high-volume centers is recommended for complex RPLND cases. Careful evaluation of pre- and post-chemotherapy imaging to ascertain vascular anomalies or anatomic variants should be performed. Strategies to facilitate enhanced recovery are encouraged including regional anesthesia and peripherally acting mu-opioid receptor antagonists [TEST-H]. Preoperative CT/MRI should be performed within 4 weeks of planned surgery with tumor markers within 2 weeks of confirmation of normal levels.

cards.name: Radical inguinal orchiectomy
cards.detail: Approach: Inguinal approach with high ligation of spermatic cord. Indications: Primary treatment for most patients with suspicious testicular mass on ultrasound [TEST-H, MS-5]. Key complications: Scrotal wound complications, potential loss of testicular function, psychological impact of body image changes. Operative mortality: Very low for this procedure.

cards.name: Nerve-sparing retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (RPLND)
cards.detail: Approach: Open or minimally invasive (laparoscopic/robotic at high-volume centers); nerve-sparing technique prioritized. Indications: Primary RPLND for stage I nonseminoma and select stage II; post-chemotherapy RPLND for residual retroperitoneal masses in nonseminoma [TEST-H, MS-18]. Key complications: Retrograde ejaculation (minimized with nerve-sparing approach), ejaculatory disorders, lymphocele. Operative mortality: Low at experienced centers.

cards.name: Testis-sparing surgery (partial orchiectomy)
cards.detail: Approach: Inguinal approach with frozen section analysis. Indications: Select patients: equivocal mass <2 cm with normal markers, synchronous bilateral tumors, solitary testicle with adequate gonadal function [TEST-H]. Key complications: Higher risk of local recurrence compared to radical orchiectomy, potential need for completion orchiectomy. Operative mortality: Very low.

cards.name: Post-chemotherapy RPLND
cards.detail: Approach: Bilateral template strongly considered; open approach at high-volume centers. Indications: Residual retroperitoneal mass (>1 cm on axial imaging) following systemic chemotherapy with normalized serum tumor markers in nonseminoma [TEST-H, MS-22]. Key complications: Vascular injury, ureteral injury, chylous ascites, retrograde ejaculation, need for adjacent structure resection. Operative mortality: Higher than primary RPLND due to complexity.

cards.name: Surgical resection of residual masses
cards.detail: Approach: Resection of all residual masses. Indications: Residual masses after second-line chemotherapy with normal markers [NSEM-9]. Key complications: Morbidity depends on location and extent of disease. Operative mortality: Variable based on clinical scenario.
